annotations.label: Encryption

anchor.snippet: which is the process of converting ordinary information

annotations.note: Encryption is an applied cryptographic process with no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cipher

anchor.snippet: is a pair of algorithms that carry out the encryption

annotations.note: No cipher abstraction exists in Mathlib; the only 'cipher' grep hit is unrelated coding theory.

annotations.label: Key

anchor.snippet: The key is a secret

annotations.note: Cryptographic keys are not modeled in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cryptosystem

anchor.snippet: is the ordered list of elements of finite possible plaintexts

annotations.note: No cryptosystem structure is defined anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetric cryptosystem

anchor.snippet: the same secret key encrypts and decrypts a message

annotations.note: Symmetric cryptosystems are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Asymmetric cryptosystem

anchor.snippet: Asymmetric systems use a "public key" to encrypt a message

annotations.note: Asymmetric/public-key cryptosystems are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Code (cryptography)

anchor.snippet: the replacement of a unit of plaintext

annotations.note: Cryptographic codebooks are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cryptanalysis

anchor.snippet: is the term used for the study of methods for obtaining the meaning of encrypted information

annotations.note: Cryptanalysis has no representation in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cryptolinguistics

anchor.snippet: is called cryptolinguistics

annotations.note: Cryptolinguistics is not a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transposition cipher

anchor.snippet: which rearrange the order of letters in a message

annotations.note: Transposition ciphers are not formalized, though permutations exist as a generic concept.

annotations.label: Substitution cipher

anchor.snippet: which systematically replace letters or groups of letters with other letters or groups of letters

annotations.note: Substitution ciphers are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Caesar cipher

anchor.snippet: in which each letter in the plaintext was replaced by a letter three positions further down the alphabet

annotations.note: The Caesar cipher is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Steganography

anchor.snippet: hiding even the existence of a message so as to keep it confidential

annotations.note: Steganography is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kautiliyam cipher

anchor.snippet: the cipher letter substitutions are based on phonetic relations

annotations.note: The Kautiliyam cipher is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polyalphabetic cipher

anchor.snippet: to use different ciphers (i.e., substitution alphabets) for various parts of a message

annotations.note: Polyalphabetic ciphers are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vigenère cipher

anchor.snippet: encryption uses a key word , which controls letter substitution

annotations.note: The Vigenère cipher is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kerckhoffs's Principle

anchor.snippet: should remain secure even if the adversary fully understands the cipher algorithm itself

annotations.note: Kerckhoffs's principle is a security design principle absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: One-time pad unconditional security

anchor.snippet: The one-time pad is one, and was proven to be so by Claude Shannon

annotations.note: Shannon's perfect-secrecy theorem for the one-time pad is not in Mathlib, despite some entropy lemmas existing.

annotations.label: RSA security from factoring

anchor.snippet: the infeasibility of factoring extremely large integers is the basis for believing that RSA is secure

annotations.note: RSA and computational-infeasibility security claims are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rabin cryptosystem provable security

anchor.snippet: that are provably secure provided factoring

annotations.note: The Rabin cryptosystem's security reduction is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetric-key cryptography

anchor.snippet: refers to encryption methods in which both the sender and receiver share the same key

annotations.note: Symmetric-key cryptography is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Block cipher

anchor.snippet: A block cipher enciphers input in blocks of plaintext

annotations.note: Block ciphers are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stream cipher

anchor.snippet: create an arbitrarily long stream of key material

annotations.note: Stream ciphers are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Message authentication code

anchor.snippet: except that a secret key can be used to authenticate the hash value upon receipt

annotations.note: Message authentication codes are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Key-count grows as square

anchor.snippet: The number of keys required increases as the square of the number of network members

annotations.note: This key-count claim is not stated as a cryptographic proposition in Mathlib, though binomial coefficients exist generically.

annotations.label: Public-key cryptography

anchor.snippet: in which two different but mathematically related keys are used

annotations.note: Public-key cryptography is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Private key infeasible from public key

anchor.snippet: calculation of one key (the 'private key') is computationally infeasible from the other

annotations.note: Computational-infeasibility of recovering the private key is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Diffie–Hellman key exchange

anchor.snippet: to allow two parties to secretly agree on a shared encryption key

annotations.note: Diffie–Hellman key exchange is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: RSA algorithm

anchor.snippet: whose solution has since become known as the RSA algorithm

annotations.note: The RSA algorithm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Digital signature scheme

anchor.snippet: there are two algorithms: one for signing

annotations.note: Digital signature schemes are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Public-key algorithms from hard problems

anchor.snippet: are most often based on the computational complexity of "hard" problems

annotations.note: Reductions of public-key schemes to hard problems are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hybrid cryptosystem

anchor.snippet: in which a fast high-quality symmetric-key encryption algorithm is used for the message itself

annotations.note: Hybrid cryptosystems are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cryptographic hash function

anchor.snippet: are functions that take a variable-length input and return a fixed-length output

annotations.note: Cryptographic hash functions are not formalized; Mathlib's 'hash' references are tactic infrastructure only.

annotations.label: Collision and preimage resistance

anchor.snippet: it must be difficult to compute two inputs that hash to the same value

annotations.note: Collision and preimage resistance are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Goal of cryptanalysis

anchor.snippet: is to find some weakness or insecurity in a cryptographic scheme

annotations.note: The goal of cryptanalysis is an informal notion absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: One-time pad is unbreakable

anchor.snippet: proved that the one-time pad cipher is unbreakable, provided the key material is truly random

annotations.note: Shannon's unbreakability theorem for the one-time pad is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ciphertext-only attack

anchor.snippet: Eve has access only to the ciphertext

annotations.note: Attack models are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Known-plaintext attack

anchor.snippet: Eve has access to a ciphertext and its corresponding plaintext

annotations.note: Known-plaintext attacks are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chosen-plaintext attack

anchor.snippet: Eve may choose a plaintext and learn its corresponding ciphertext

annotations.note: Chosen-plaintext attacks are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chosen-ciphertext attack

anchor.snippet: Eve may be able to choose ciphertexts and learn their corresponding plaintexts

annotations.note: Chosen-ciphertext attacks are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Man-in-the-middle attack

anchor.snippet: Eve gets in between Alice (the sender) and Bob (the recipient)

annotations.note: Man-in-the-middle attacks are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brute-force attack on DES

anchor.snippet: a simple brute force attack against DES requires one known plaintext

annotations.note: DES and brute-force attack complexity are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear cryptanalysis of DES

anchor.snippet: a linear cryptanalysis attack against DES requires

annotations.note: Linear cryptanalysis is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: RSA needs larger keys than ECC

anchor.snippet: require larger keys than elliptic curve techniques

annotations.note: This key-size comparison is a cryptographic claim absent from Mathlib, which only formalizes elliptic curves as algebraic objects.

annotations.label: Side-channel attack

anchor.snippet: based on actual use of the algorithms in real devices

annotations.note: Side-channel attacks are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Timing attack

anchor.snippet: use a timing attack to break a cipher that is otherwise resistant to analysis

annotations.note: Timing attacks are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Traffic analysis

anchor.snippet: study the pattern and length of messages to derive valuable information

annotations.note: Traffic analysis is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cryptographic primitive

anchor.snippet: algorithms with basic cryptographic properties

annotations.note: Cryptographic primitives are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cryptosystem

anchor.snippet: are often used to develop a more complex algorithm, called a cryptographic system

annotations.note: Cryptographic systems are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lightweight cryptography

anchor.snippet: concerns cryptographic algorithms developed for a strictly constrained environment

annotations.note: Lightweight cryptography is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Password hashing

anchor.snippet: the system passes the given password through a cryptographic hash function and compares it to the hashed value on file

annotations.note: Password hashing is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantum threat to public-key keys

anchor.snippet: a quantum computer could reduce the effort required to break today’s strongest RSA or elliptic-curve keys

annotations.note: Quantum attacks on public-key cryptography (e.g. Shor's algorithm) are not formalized in Mathlib.
